tests.description: a valid URL with anchor tag
a valid URL with anchor tag and parentheses
a valid URL with URL-encoded stuff
a valid URL with many special characters
a valid URL based on IPv4
a valid URL with ftp scheme
a valid URL for a simple text file
an invalid URI though valid URI reference
an invalid URI with spaces and missing scheme
an invalid URI with comma in scheme
URI with leading-zero IPv4 is structurally valid as a reg-name
tests.comment: RFC 3986, Section 3.2.2: If an IP literal fails strict IPv4address parsing, it falls back to reg-name. A string of digits and dots is valid under unreserved characters. JSON Schema format asserts syntax, not scheme-specific DNS semantics.

tests.description: URI with out-of-bounds IPv4 is structurally valid as a reg-name
tests.comment: RFC 3986, Section 3.2.2: Fallback to reg-name allows digits and dots.

tests.description: scheme must start with a letter
minimum valid URI is scheme and colon
scheme with digits after first character
scheme with all special scheme characters
tilde is not valid in scheme
space is not valid in scheme
slash is not valid in scheme
equals is not valid in scheme
scheme cannot be empty before colon
valid percent-encoding with max hex value
invalid percent-encoding with non-hex letter G
all unreserved characters are valid in path
all sub-delimiters are valid in path
colon is valid in path segment
at-sign is valid in path segment
close brace is invalid in path
control character is invalid in path
userinfo may be a single character
userinfo may contain colon-delimited password text
userinfo may contain all unreserved characters
userinfo slash boundary: slash starts path
tests.comment: RFC 3986 parses the authority as host `u`; `/s@a.com` is the path, so the URI is syntactically valid.

tests.description: userinfo question boundary: question mark starts query
tests.comment: RFC 3986 parses the authority as host `u`; `s@a.com` is the query, so the URI is syntactically valid.

tests.description: host may be a single-character reg-name
host reg-name may contain multiple subdomains
host reg-name may end with a dot
host with too few IPv4 octets is structurally valid as a reg-name
host with too many IPv4 octets is structurally valid as a reg-name
host IPv6 with invalid hex digits
host IPvFuture empty content after dot
port may be empty when colon is present
port may be a single digit
port may be a typical value
port has no RFC 3986 upper bound
port with leading plus is invalid
port with leading minus is invalid
port with decimal point is invalid
a valid URL with every major component present
port digit boundary: forward slash terminates port and starts path
tests.comment: RFC 3986 section 3.2.3: '/' is a delimiter after port; this parses as port=8 and path=/0.

tests.description: port digit boundary: one above range (colon)
tests.comment: RFC 3986 section 3.2.3: ':' is not a digit; extra ':' after host:port makes authority invalid.

tests.description: authority/path law: double slash selects authority form
tests.comment: RFC 3986 section 3: '//...' selects hier-part authority form in generic syntax.

tests.description: authority/path law: empty authority with absolute path
tests.comment: RFC 3986 section 3.3: empty authority followed by path-abempty is valid.

tests.description: wrong-format string: date-time passed as URI
tests.comment: Format-specificity check: this is not a URI.
RFC 3986 has no generic maximum length; this remains syntactically valid.

tests.description: above-ceiling DIGIT in port: colon is %x3A, one above 9 (%x39)
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.3: port = *DIGIT; ':' is not a DIGIT, so extra ':' after host:port is invalid | layer: 1

tests.description: slash (%x2F) is below HEXDIG floor in percent-encoding
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §2.1: pct-encoded = '%' HEXDIG HEXDIG; '/' is not HEXDIG, so this percent-encoding is invalid | layer: 1

tests.description: Unicode decimal digit in port is not DIGIT
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.3: port = *DIGIT; non-ASCII decimal digits do not match the port grammar | layer: 1

tests.description: IPvFuture with uppercase V is valid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: IPvFuture uses literal 'v'; ABNF string literals are case-insensitive, so uppercase 'V' is valid | layer: 1

tests.description: IPv6 address with all groups at minimum one hex digit is valid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: h16 = 1*4HEXDIG; 8 one-digit groups match the full IPv6address form | layer: 1

tests.description: IPv6 group with five hex digits exceeds h16 maximum of four
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: h16 = 1*4HEXDIG; five hex digits in one group exceed the h16 maximum | layer: 1

tests.description: IPv6 address with two double-colons is invalid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: each IPv6address alternative allows at most one '::'; two occurrences match no alternative | layer: 1

tests.description: IPv6 address with seven groups and no double-colon is invalid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: without '::', the full IPv6address form requires 8 groups; 7 groups are invalid | layer: 1

tests.description: IPv6 address with nine groups is invalid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: no IPv6address alternative allows more than 8 groups; 9 groups are invalid | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet = DIGIT / ... — alt1 is a bare DIGIT, range 0-9; 9 is the ceiling of this alternative | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet alt2 = %x31-39 DIGIT — starts at 10 (first two-digit value); this is the floor of alt2 | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet alt2 ceiling is 99 — %x39 DIGIT = '9' followed by any digit | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet alt3 = '1' 2DIGIT — starts at 100; this is the floor of alt3 | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet alt3 ceiling is 199 — '1' followed by two nines | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet alt4 = '2' %x30-34 DIGIT — starts at 200; '2' + '0' + any DIGIT | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet alt4 ceiling is 249 — '2' + %x34 ('4') + '9' | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.2.2: dec-octet alt5 = '25' %x30-35 — starts at 250; this is also the transition point where alt4 ends and alt5 begins | layer: 1

tests.description: at-sign is valid in query because pchar includes at-sign
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.4: query = *(pchar / '/' / '?'); pchar includes '@'; at-sign is valid in query — symmetry with path test | layer: 1

tests.description: at-sign is valid in fragment because pchar includes at-sign
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.5: fragment = *(pchar / '/' / '?'); pchar includes '@'; at-sign is valid in fragment — symmetry with path test | layer: 1

tests.description: open brace is invalid in query for same reason as in path
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.4: query = *(pchar / '/' / '?'); '{' is not in pchar, '/', or '?'; same exclusion as path — proves forbidden charset applies universally across components | layer: 1

tests.description: open brace is invalid in fragment for same reason as in path
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.5: fragment = *(pchar / '/' / '?'); '{' is not in pchar, '/', or '?'; same exclusion as path | layer: 1

tests.description: empty path with query directly after authority is valid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.3: path-abempty = *('/' segment); '*' allows zero repetitions; query follows the authority immediately without any path slash | layer: 1

tests.description: empty path with fragment directly after authority is valid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.3: path-abempty may be empty (zero repetitions); fragment follows authority directly | layer: 1

tests.description: colon in first segment makes scheme separator, yielding valid URI
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3: 'this:that' parses as scheme='this', path-rootless='that' — a valid URI, not a relative-ref; distinguishes uri from uri-reference where path-noscheme forbids colon in first segment | layer: 1

tests.description: IPv6 trailing double-colon is valid with prefix groups
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: IPv6address includes [ *6( h16 ':' ) h16 ] '::', so a valid prefix followed by trailing '::' is valid | layer: 1

tests.description: port with leading zero is valid under unrestricted *DIGIT port
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.3: port = *DIGIT; ABNF places no restriction on leading zeros — '080' is three DIGIT characters and matches the production | layer: 1

tests.description: tilde is valid in reg-name because it is an unreserved character
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: reg-name = *(unreserved / pct-encoded / sub-delims); unreserved = ALPHA / DIGIT / '-' / '.' / '_' / '~'; tilde tested in host context (also valid in path, userinfo per reused unreserved production) | layer: 1

tests.description: underscore is valid in reg-name because it is an unreserved character
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: reg-name allows unreserved characters, and unreserved includes '_' | layer: 1

tests.description: two at-signs in authority leave trailing @ and make URI invalid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.1: userinfo = *( unreserved / pct-encoded / sub-delims / ':' ); '@' is not in this set — the first '@' terminates userinfo='user'; host parses as 'pass' (stops at second '@'); '@a.com' is then unconsumed (not a valid path/query/fragment delimiter) — URI invalid. Guards against implementations that scan for the last '@' to split userinfo | layer: 1

RFC 3986 §3.2.2 + §2.1: reg-name = *( unreserved / pct-encoded / sub-delims ); pct-encoded = '%' HEXDIG HEXDIG; 'G' (%x47) is not in HEXDIG (%x41-46); reg-name parse fails at the '%'; URI invalid. Invalid pct-encoding was tested in path, query, fragment, and userinfo — this closes the reg-name context | layer: 1

tests.description: path-absolute consisting of a single slash is valid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.3: path-absolute = '/' [ segment-nz *('/' segment) ]; the optional segment-nz may be absent — '/' alone is a valid path-absolute | layer: 1

tests.description: path-rootless URI may carry both a query and a fragment
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3: URI = scheme ':' hier-part ['?' query] ['#' fragment]; hier-part may be path-rootless; both optional suffixes may be present simultaneously | layer: 1

tests.description: authority with empty host and explicit port is valid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2.2: host = reg-name = *( unreserved / pct-encoded / sub-delims ); '*' allows zero chars — empty host is valid; port = *DIGIT = '80' follows normally | layer: 1

tests.description: userinfo with non-empty value and empty host is valid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.2: authority = [userinfo '@'] host [':' port]; userinfo='user', '@' present, host=reg-name='' (empty reg-name is valid), port absent | layer: 1

tests.description: query invalid percent-encoding with non-hex in first nibble
fragment invalid percent-encoding with non-hex in first nibble
userinfo invalid percent-encoding with non-hex in first nibble
reg-name incomplete percent-encoding with one hex digit
path-empty non-authority with query is valid
path-empty non-authority with fragment is valid
path-empty non-authority with query and fragment is valid
IPvFuture with non-hex version is invalid

tests.comment: RFC 3986 §3.3: segment = *pchar; pchar = unreserved / pct-encoded / sub-delims / ':' / '@'; '[' (%x5B) and ']' (%x5D) are gen-delims — not in any pchar alternative | layer: 1
RFC 3986 §3.5: fragment = *(pchar / '/' / '?'); '[' and ']' are gen-delims not in pchar, '/', or '?' — same exclusion as path and query | layer: 1
RFC 3986 Section 3.2.3: port = *DIGIT; there is no pct-encoded alternative, so percent-encoded digits are invalid here.
RFC 3986 Section 3.1: scheme = ALPHA *( ALPHA / DIGIT / '+' / '-' / '.' ); there is no pct-encoded alternative in scheme.

tests.description: IPv6 with embedded IPv4 containing out-of-range octet is invalid
tests.comment: RFC 3986 Section 3.2.2: IPv6address ls32 alternatives reuse IPv4address; dec-octet does not allow 256, so the embedded IPv4 must fail.
